```{r fig-7, fig.cap="Correlation Matrix of Created Risk Metrics"}
# Create the heatmap
ggplot(melted_cor, aes(Var1, Var2, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#6D9EC1", high = "#E46726", mid = "white", 
                       midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1,1), space = "Lab",
                       name = "Correlation") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(value, 2)), size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)) +
  coord_fixed()
```

The following section presents tables of airport rankings based on the metrics calculated by this simulation. For the results, a top-60 cutoff by weekly flight volume was selected to include my home airport, Bradley International Airport (ICAO: KBDL) in Hartford, Connecticut.

```{r centrality-table, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

final_thesis_metrics |>
  slice_max(order_by = weekly_flights, n = 60) |>
  dplyr::select(
    Airport = departure,
    `Weekly Flights` = weekly_flights,
    `Hub Centrality` = hub_centrality
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  arrange(desc(`Hub Centrality`)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Airports by Network Centrality (PageRank)"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"))
```

The most influential airports in the network were identified using PageRank centrality, a classic network analysis index. The Hub Centrality scores are normalized to a mean of 1.0, and scale linearly (e.g., a score of 2.5 means that the airport is 150% more central than average). Unsurprisingly, the largest hub airports in the United States are the leaders of this index.

```{r exp-value-delay-table, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

final_thesis_metrics |>
  slice_max(order_by = weekly_flights, n = 60) |>
  dplyr::select(Airport = departure, 
                `Expected Value Delay` = expected_value_delay,
                `Utilization` = rho) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  arrange(desc(`Expected Value Delay`)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Airports by Expected Queuing Delay (Among 60 Busiest)"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"))
```

The theoretical expected queuing delay is derived from M/G/1 queuing and the Pollaczek-Khinchine formula. The Expected Value Delay represents the average additional waiting time flights experience beyond their scheduled slot due to capacity constraints and demand fluctuations. These airports are very different from the mega-hubs above; instead, they are all relatively small spokes.

```{r fragile-table, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

final_thesis_metrics |>
  slice_max(order_by = weekly_flights, n = 60) |>
  dplyr::select(
    Airport = departure,
    `Fragility Index` = fragility_index,
    `Expected Delay` = expected_value_delay,
    `Delay Variance` = expected_variance_delay,
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  arrange(desc(`Fragility Index`)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Most Fragile Airports by Variance-to-Mean Ratio (Among 60 Busiest)"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"))
```

The fragility identifies airports where delay variability is disproportionately high relative to average delay. High fragility indicates that these airports may function adequately under typical conditions but experience explosive delay growth in response to minor utilization swings. Airports can appear fragile through different mechanisms: either extremely high variance from near-capacity operations (not the case with these relatively large airports) or moderate variance combined with very low baseline delays. This metric can show which airports require buffer capacity or demand management.

```{r avg-downstream-table, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

final_thesis_metrics |>
  slice_max(order_by = weekly_flights, n = 60) |>
  dplyr::select(Airport = departure, 
                `Expected Value Delay` = expected_value_delay,
                `Avg Downstream Impact` = avg_downstream_impact, 
                `Network Weighted Delay` = network_weighted_delay) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  arrange(desc(`Avg Downstream Impact`)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Airports by Average Downstream Impact (Among 60 Busiest)"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"))
```

The downstream impact quantifies how many subsequent flights in an aircraft's daily rotation depend on each departing flight. High downstream-impact airports are those where aircraft tend to be early in their flights, so a delay can cascade through multiple subsequent segments. Airports serving as overnight bases or morning departure hubs naturally rank high because aircraft leave with full days of flying ahead. The comparison with the Expected Value Delay shows whether high-impact airports also experience local congestion.

```{r network-weighted-table, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

final_thesis_metrics |>
  slice_max(order_by = weekly_flights, n = 60) |>
  dplyr::select(
    Airport = departure,
    `Network Weighted Delay` = network_weighted_delay,
    `Expected Value Delay` = expected_value_delay,
    `Avg Downstream Impact` = avg_downstream_impact,
    `Hub Centrality` = hub_centrality
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  arrange(desc(`Network Weighted Delay`)) |>
  slice_head(n = 20) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Airports by Network-Weighted Delay (Among 60 Busiest)"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"))
```

The composite metric Network Weighted Delay combines local congestion risk with a normalization of downstream propagation and centrality weighting. The ranking represents the most holistic assessment of which airports, when considering their internal delay and network amplification effects together. The normalization process ensures that both components contribute meaningfully regardless of their original scales. Airports at the top of this ranking are prime candidates for airlines to reconsider their strategies, since improvements here yield disproportionate network-wide benefits.

```{r delay-sensitivity-scenario}

scenarios <- list(
  
  baseline = list(
    name = "Baseline",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.08, meanlog = 2.5, sdlog = 0.8),
      nas = list(prob = 0.05, shape = 2.0, rate = 0.1),
      weather = list(prob = 0.03, shape = 1.5, rate = 0.05)
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 1.0,
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat, rate = rate_hat)
  ),
  
  high_carrier_delays = list(
    name = "High Carrier Delays",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.15, meanlog = 3.0, sdlog = 1.0),  # Increased
      nas = list(prob = 0.05, shape = 2.0, rate = 0.1),
      weather = list(prob = 0.03, shape = 1.5, rate = 0.05)
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 1.0,
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat, rate = rate_hat)
  ),
  
  severe_weather = list(
    name = "Severe Weather",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.08, meanlog = 2.5, sdlog = 0.8),
      nas = list(prob = 0.05, shape = 2.0, rate = 0.1),
      weather = list(prob = 0.12, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.04)  # More frequent & severe
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 1.0,
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat, rate = rate_hat)
  ),
  
  nas_congestion = list(
    name = "NAS Congestion",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.08, meanlog = 2.5, sdlog = 0.8),
      nas = list(prob = 0.12, shape = 3.0, rate = 0.08),  # More NAS delays
      weather = list(prob = 0.03, shape = 1.5, rate = 0.05)
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 1.0,
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat, rate = rate_hat)
  ),
  
  reduced_capacity = list(
    name = "Reduced Capacity (-20%)",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.08, meanlog = 2.5, sdlog = 0.8),
      nas = list(prob = 0.05, shape = 2.0, rate = 0.1),
      weather = list(prob = 0.03, shape = 1.5, rate = 0.05)
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 0.8,  # 20% capacity reduction
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat, rate = rate_hat)
  ),
  
  slow_turnaround = list(
    name = "Slow Turnaround",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.08, meanlog = 2.5, sdlog = 0.8),
      nas = list(prob = 0.05, shape = 2.0, rate = 0.1),
      weather = list(prob = 0.03, shape = 1.5, rate = 0.05)
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 1.0,
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat * 1.3, rate = rate_hat * 0.9)  # Slower
  ),
  
  compound_stress = list(
    name = "Compound Stress",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.12, meanlog = 2.8, sdlog = 0.9),
      nas = list(prob = 0.08, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.09),
      weather = list(prob = 0.06, shape = 2.0, rate = 0.045)
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 0.85,  # 15% capacity reduction
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat * 1.2, rate = rate_hat * 0.95)
  ),
  
  optimistic = list(
    name = "Optimistic",
    delays = list(
      carrier = list(prob = 0.05, meanlog = 2.0, sdlog = 0.6),
      nas = list(prob = 0.03, shape = 1.5, rate = 0.12),
      weather = list(prob = 0.02, shape = 1.2, rate = 0.06)
    ),
    capacity_multiplier = 1.1,  # 10% capacity increase
    turnaround = list(shape = shape_hat * 0.9, rate = rate_hat * 1.1)
  )
)

run_scenario_simulation <- function(schedule, airport_info_base, scenario_config, seed = 115) {
  
  # Adjust airport capacities based on scenario
  airport_info <- airport_info_base |>
    mutate(
      capacity_per_hour = capacity_per_hour * scenario_config$capacity_multiplier
    )
  
  # Generate delays based on scenario parameters
  n <- nrow(schedule)
  
  carrier_delays <- generate_carrier_delay_custom(
    n,
    prob = scenario_config$delays$carrier$prob,
    meanlog = scenario_config$delays$carrier$meanlog,
    sdlog = scenario_config$delays$carrier$sdlog
  )
  
  nas_delays <- generate_nas_delay_custom(
    n,
    prob = scenario_config$delays$nas$prob,
    shape = scenario_config$delays$nas$shape,
    rate = scenario_config$delays$nas$rate
  )
  
  weather_delays <- generate_weather_delay_custom(
    n,
    prob = scenario_config$delays$weather$prob,
    shape = scenario_config$delays$weather$shape,
    rate = scenario_config$delays$weather$rate
  )
  
  # Calculate total delay and affected flights
  total_delay <- carrier_delays + nas_delays + weather_delays
  affected_flights <- sum(total_delay > 0)
  
  # Calculate theoretical risk with adjusted capacity
  theoretical_risk <- calculate_theoretical_airport_risk(schedule, airport_info)
  
  # Calculate network risk
  network_risk <- calculate_network_weighted_risk(schedule, theoretical_risk)
  
  # Summary metrics
  list(
    scenario = scenario_config$name,
    total_system_delay = sum(total_delay),
    affected_flights = affected_flights,
    pct_affected = affected_flights / n * 100,
    mean_delay_all = mean(total_delay),
    mean_delay_affected = mean(total_delay[total_delay > 0]),
    median_delay_affected = median(total_delay[total_delay > 0]),
    p95_delay = quantile(total_delay, 0.95),
    p99_delay = quantile(total_delay, 0.99),
    carrier_contribution = sum(carrier_delays) / sum(total_delay) * 100,
    nas_contribution = sum(nas_delays) / sum(total_delay) * 100,
    weather_contribution = sum(weather_delays) / sum(total_delay) * 100,
    avg_utilization = mean(theoretical_risk$rho, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_utilization = max(theoretical_risk$rho, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_expected_delay = mean(theoretical_risk$expected_value_delay, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_fragility = mean(theoretical_risk$fragility_index, na.rm = TRUE),
    network_risk = network_risk,
    theoretical_risk = theoretical_risk
  )
}

# Custom delay generators that accept parameters
generate_carrier_delay_custom <- function(n, prob, meanlog, sdlog) {
  delays <- rep(0, n)
  affected <- runif(n) < prob
  if (sum(affected) > 0) {
    delays[affected] <- rlnorm(sum(affected), meanlog = meanlog, sdlog = sdlog)
  }
  pmin(round(delays), 180)
}

generate_nas_delay_custom <- function(n, prob, shape, rate) {
  delays <- rep(0, n)
  affected <- runif(n) < prob
  if (sum(affected) > 0) {
    delays[affected] <- rgamma(sum(affected), shape = shape, rate = rate)
  }
  pmin(round(delays), 180)
}

generate_weather_delay_custom <- function(n, prob, shape, rate) {
  delays <- rep(0, n)
  affected <- runif(n) < prob
  if (sum(affected) > 0) {
    delays[affected] <- rgamma(sum(affected), shape = shape, rate = rate)
  }
  pmin(round(delays), 180)
}

set.seed(115)
scenario_results <- map_dfr(scenarios, function(scenario) {
  result <- run_scenario_simulation(final_formatted, airport_info, scenario)
  
  tibble(
    Scenario = result$scenario,
    `Total Delay (min)` = result$total_system_delay,
    `Flights Affected (%)` = result$pct_affected,
    `Mean Delay (affected)` = result$mean_delay_affected,
    `P95 Delay` = result$p95_delay,
    `P99 Delay` = result$p99_delay,
    `Avg ρ` = result$avg_utilization,
    `Max ρ` = result$max_utilization,
    `Avg E[Delay]` = result$avg_expected_delay,
    `Avg Fragility` = result$avg_fragility
  )
})
```

```{r scenario-overview, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

baseline_total_delay <- scenario_results |>
  filter(Scenario == "Baseline") |>
  pull(`Total Delay (min)`)

table1 <- scenario_results |>
  select(
    Scenario,
    `Total Delay (min)`,
    `Flights Affected (%)`,
    `Mean Delay (affected)`
  )

table2 <- scenario_results |>
  mutate(
    `Delay Change (%)` = (`Total Delay (min)` - baseline_total_delay) / baseline_total_delay * 100,
    `Delay Multiplier` = `Total Delay (min)` / baseline_total_delay
  ) |>
  select(
    Scenario,
    `P95 Delay`,
    `P99 Delay`,
    `Delay Multiplier`,
    `Avg E[Delay]`,
    `Avg Fragility`
  )

table1 |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "System-Wide Risk Metrics"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"))

table2 |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "System-Wide Risk Metrics"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"))
```

The metrics from eight operational scenarios, ranging from optimistic conditions to compound stress, are shown, each varying the aforementioned delay distributions. Some scenarios only isolate specific delay factors, while other compounding scenarios demonstrate how multiple simultaneous pressures create nonlinear effects. The metrics span multiple dimensions, from overall flight delays to more granular details about the upper end of delays, and the utilization and queuing behavior under each scenario.

```{r delay-source-breakdown, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

delay_source_results <- map_dfr(scenarios, function(scenario) {
  result <- run_scenario_simulation(final_formatted, airport_info, scenario)
  
  tibble(
    Scenario = result$scenario,
    `Carrier (%)` = result$carrier_contribution,
    `NAS (%)` = result$nas_contribution,
    `Weather (%)` = result$weather_contribution,
    `Total Delay` = result$total_system_delay
  )
})

delay_source_results |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 1))) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Delay Source Contribution by Scenario"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"))
```

Total delays across the scenarios were broken down into their sources: carrier operations (i.e., maintenance, crew, airline decisions), National Airspace System congestion (i.e., air traffic control, routing), and weather. The percentage breakdowns reveal how different scenarios shift the relative importance of delay mechanisms.

```{r baseline-stress-comparison, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

baseline_result <- run_scenario_simulation(final_formatted, airport_info, scenarios$baseline)
stress_result <- run_scenario_simulation(final_formatted, airport_info, scenarios$compound_stress)

airport_comparison <- baseline_result$network_risk |>
  slice_max(order_by = weekly_flights, n = 60) |>
  arrange(desc(network_weighted_delay)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(Airport = departure, 
         `Baseline E[Delay]` = expected_value_delay
         ) |>
  left_join(
    stress_result$network_risk |>
      select(departure, 
             `Stress E[Delay]` = expected_value_delay
             ),
    by = c("Airport" = "departure")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    `E[Delay] Change (%)` = (`Stress E[Delay]` - `Baseline E[Delay]`) / `Baseline E[Delay]` * 100,
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 2)))

airport_comparison |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Top 10 Airports: Baseline vs Compound Stress Scenario"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"),
    font_size = 9
  )
```

This analysis compares airports between normal operations and extreme stress. Airports with large percentage increases demonstrate fragility, and potential;y their queuing dynamics exhibit nonlinear behavior with utilization increases triggering exponential delay growth.

```{r hub-class-comparison, results = 'asis'}
cat("\\clearpage\n")
cat("\\thispagestyle{empty}\n")

hub_class_comparison <- map_dfr(c("baseline", "compound_stress", "reduced_capacity"), function(scenario_name) {
  result <- run_scenario_simulation(final_formatted, airport_info, scenarios[[scenario_name]])

  result$theoretical_risk |>
    mutate(hub_class = case_when(
      hub_class == "non_hub" ~ "Non Hub",
      hub_class == "small_hub" ~ "Small Hub",
      hub_class == "medium_hub" ~ "Medium Hub",
      hub_class == "large_hub" ~ "Large Hub",
      TRUE ~ hub_class
    )) |>
    group_by(hub_class) |>
    summarise(
      `Mean Utilization` = mean(rho, na.rm = TRUE),
      `Mean E[Delay]` = mean(expected_value_delay, na.rm = TRUE),
      `Mean Fragility` = mean(fragility_index, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    mutate(Scenario = scenarios[[scenario_name]]$name) |>
    dplyr::select(Scenario, `Hub Class` = hub_class, everything())
})

hub_class_comparison |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))) |>
  arrange(`Hub Class`, Scenario) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Hub Class Performance Across Key Scenarios"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"))
```

Airports of different sizes respond differently to operational stress, with varying levels of capacity influencing fragility. This cross-scenario comparison shows how the hub class hierarchy remains stable under stress scenarios and can inform resource allocation decisions.

\pagebreak

## Conclusion

This analysis demonstrates that open-source aviation data, while creating research transparency and reproducibility, presents pre-processing challenges. The OpenSky Network’s data is now more usable for future work after gaining experience with what it offers, and the frameworks here could serve as a baseline for other cleaning of this open-source data.

Network theory reveals an asymmetry in how airports may contribute to system performance: either propagating delays downstream through aircraft rotations or absorbing them with a schedule buffer. However, the network-weighted metrics demonstrate that local congestion severity does not directly translate to systemic importance. Instead, airports rank highly when they are both capacity-constrained (experiencing high local queuing delay) and deeply embedded in the network (exhibiting a high number of downstream flights and PageRank centrality), meaning disruptions at these hubs generate disproportionately large system-wide delays. Smaller regional airports may exhibit higher utilization and longer queuing delays due to limited infrastructure, yet their peripheral position in the network contains disruptions locally. In contrast, major hubs operating at low utilization levels can absorb local delays without them spreading far beyond.

This analysis exposes the central paradox of hub-and-spoke aviation networks: the very feature that enables efficient connectivity—with high traffic at strategically positioned hubs over concentrated time periods—simultaneously creates a systemic vulnerability. Under normal operations, they optimize aircraft utilization, maximize connections, and generate the network economies and economies of scale that make modern air travel viable. Understanding which airports sit at the junction of high local fragility and network centrality provides a data-driven foundation for infrastructure investments and schedule pattern adjustments that can protect the network even when individual nodes experience stress.

Further, different operational scenarios illustrate how delay dynamics shift as the system’s delays change scenarios. Under baseline scenarios, delays are relatively balanced across carrier operations, NAS constraints, and weather. However, as stressors are introduced, these factors interact to produce nonlinear increases in total delay, showing that disruptions are interdependent rather than additive.

\pagebreak

## References
